summary: Obfuscated Windows backdoor with persistence, process masquerade, and browser surveillance
details: On every launch, launcher.js eval()s an 81 KB obfuscator.io-packed server.obf.js (string-array rotation, _0x identifiers) — the package's actual logic is shipped as an opaque blob to defeat review. It also loads sens.node, a 6.8 MB native addon with no source or build script, via a monkey-patched Module._load that rewrites the addon's filename to 'program module' in every log and error path. The package establishes Windows persistence by writing HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\OneDriveUpdate (executed via execSync of a powershell Set-ItemProperty command) pointing at a sibling OneDrive.Standalone.Updater.vbs that invokes wscript.exe to relaunch `node.exe launcher.js` with a 0-window flag — a deliberate impersonation of a legitimate Microsoft updater. process.title is set to 'Runtime Broker' to mimic the Windows RuntimeBroker.exe system process in Task Manager, and log output replaces 'sens.node' with 'program module' to hide the addon's real name. Every 3 seconds the launcher writes a temp.yt.ps1, spawns `powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass` to enumerate chrome.exe processes and grep MainWindowTitle for 'YouTube', and on a hit displays a QR code linking to http://192.168.1.16:3000 for a phone-pairing remote-control flow. The combination — obfuscated payload eval, opaque native addon, branded autostart persistence, system-process masquerade, log scrubbing, browser-window surveillance, and an undisclosed phone-pairing channel — is malware tradecraft.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
